frozen_components: pretrained GPT-2 XL and GPT-J base models (except single targeted MLP layer weights in ROME)
architecture.summary: The system combines two causal analysis methods on autoregressive transformers (GPT-2, GPT-J). First, Causal Tracing uses paired interventions (clean run, corrupted run, corrupted-with-restoration run) to measure indirect effects of hidden states on factual predictions, identifying mid-layer MLP modules at the subject's final token as decisive. Second, Rank-One Model Editing (ROME) treats MLP layers as linear associative memories, computing a rank-one weight update to insert new key-value pairs (subject→object) directly into feed-forward weights, targeting the identified causal sites.

biblio.title: Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT

claims.statement: Factual associations in GPT correspond to localized, directly-editable computations in mid-layer feed-forward MLP modules at the final token of the subject entity
Average Indirect Effect of MLP contributions at the early site (last subject token) peaks at 6.6%
ROME achieves 99.8% efficacy on zero-shot relation extraction (zsRE) task

claims.dataset: CounterFact (7,500 test records for GPT-2 XL, 2,000 for GPT-J)
claims.statement: On CounterFact dataset, ROME achieves harmonic mean Score of 89.2 on GPT-2 XL and 91.5 on GPT-J, combining efficacy, generalization, and specificity
ROME demonstrates both generalization and specificity simultaneously on counterfactual assertions, whereas fine-tuning (FT) and hypernetwork methods (KE, MEND) sacrifice one or the other
ROME's Neighborhood Score (specificity) on CounterFact is 75.4% for GPT-2 XL and 78.9% for GPT-J, compared to FT+L at 70.3% and 78.6%
ROME edits generalize best at middle layers (peak at layer 18 for GPT-2 XL) and specifically at the final subject token position, confirming causal tracing findings
ROME introduces some loss in fluency; evaluators rated ROME as 1.3 times less likely to be more fluent than FT+L, though 1.8 times more likely to be consistent with inserted facts
